title-group.article-title: COMPREHENSIVE ADOLESCENT HEALTH PROGRAMS THAT INCLUDE SEXUAL AND REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH SERVICES: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW

article-meta.aff: a Department of Population, Family and Reproductive Health, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Baltimore, MD, USA.

b UNDP/UNFPA/UNICEF/WHO/The World Bank Special Programme of Research, Development and Research Training in Human Reproduction (HRP), Department of Reproductive Health and Research, World Health Organization, Geneva, Switzerland

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding Author Contact Information Anna Kågesten, 615 N. Wolfe Street, Baltimore, MD 21205, USA, Phone: 443-838-6044, akaagesten@jhu.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To describe the characteristics and evidence of comprehensive adolescent health (CAH) programs encompassing sexual and reproductive and other health services, educational and social support.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Systematic review of peer-reviewed and grey literature on CAH programs (1998-2013). Only programs with experimental, quasi-experimental or pre-post evaluations were included. Two independent reviewers screened 36,119 records and extracted articles using predefined criteria. Data was synthesised into descriptive characteristics; quality was assessed by evidence level (rigorous, strong, modest).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Data was extracted on 46 programs (181 studies) of which 19 programs were defined as comprehensive; 9 were excluded due to insufficient evaluations. Ten CAH programs met all inclusion criteria. Most were US based; others were implemented in Egypt, Ethiopia and Mexico. Few programs (N=3) displayed rigorous evidence, 5 had strong and 2 had modest evidence. All programs with rigorous/strong evidence directly or indirectly influenced adolescent sexual and reproductive health. Common elements of these programs included: long-term commitment to adolescents, interpersonal connections, community mobilization, and skill-building elements.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The long term impact of many CAH programs cannot be proven due to insufficient evaluations. Evaluation approaches that take into account the complex operating conditions of many programs are needed to better understand mechanisms behind program effects.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Global evaluation research has repeatedly shown that the evidence and effectiveness of many adolescent pregnancy prevention programs is unclear. 1,2 Kirby (2007) found that numerous pregnancy prevention programs had little effect; however, among programs that were most successful, the majority used comprehensive approaches that combined activities related to health, education and social supports. 2,3 Both in the United States and globally, interest has turned to such comprehensive (sometimes also referred to as ‘multi-level’ or ‘multi-component’) strategies for improving the sexual and reproductive health of adolescents, in addition to overall health and developmental outcomes. In the United States, the Obama Administration has identified the Harlem Children's Zone as a prototype for its Promise Neighbourhoods that aim to improve child and adolescent education and development in disadvantaged neighbourhoods. 4 Internationally, the United Nations Population Fund and other organizations have worked together to scale up a comprehensive model for preventing early marriage and reducing adolescent fertility in 12 countries. 5

Comprehensive approaches move beyond sexuality education curriculums, contraceptive distribution programs, and abstinence-only programs to build a package of services and programs that target the root causes of sexual health risk and early pregnancy. Such strategies are based on an understanding of health and development with which adolescents are nested in a network of contexts (peers, community, health services, schools and families), suggesting that comprehensive approaches hold the greatest promise. However, little is known about the range, scope, or common activities of such programs and whether such strategies can successfully improve adolescent sexual and reproductive health.

This review explores what is known about the elements and evidence base of comprehensive adolescent health programs that encompass sexual and reproductive and other health services, educational, and social support services. The objectives of the review are to: 1) describe the characteristics of evaluated comprehensive adolescent health programs as defined below; 2) provide an assessment of the quality and evidence of comprehensive adolescent health programs; 3) identify the common elements of the strongest evaluated comprehensive adolescent health programs that show successful outcomes.

sec.title: Protocol and registration
sec.p: Methods for screening, inclusion and analysis for the current review were specified in advance and documented in a protocol (available upon request) based on WHO's systematic review practices. 6 The review was conceptualized and carried out in accordance with the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analyses) statement. 7

sec.title: Criteria for considering studies for this review
sec.p: Peer-reviewed and grey literatures between January 1998 and April 2013 were reviewed. Peer-reviewed articles were included without language restriction while the grey literature was restricted to English, Spanish and French.

sec.title: Types of participants
sec.p: The majority of program participants (80%) had to be adolescent males or females aged 10-19; however, we also considered programs targeting youth up to 24 years old. Programs had to have at least 100 participants.

sec.title: Types of programs
sec.p:
Studies included in this review had to refer to comprehensive adolescent health programs. For the purpose of this review, we define comprehensive programs as interventions that provide: 1)
Health services: including sexual and reproductive health services and at least one of the following: primary medical care, mental health, substance use counselling or services for physical/ sexual abuse. Because early pregnancy within or outside of marriage forecloses opportunities for many adolescents, delaying pregnancy and first births is central in helping young women and men achieve their potential. 1 Consequently, comprehensive adolescent health programs needed to include sexual and reproductive health (SRH) as part of the health services package offered for the purpose of this review.2)
Educational support: matriculation, retention and school completion supports; after-school programs, or services targeting out-of-school adolescents. Much like delaying marriage and early childbearing, educational achievement is critical to the health and well being of adolescents worldwide; 1,5,8,9 thus, activities to enhance school retention and academic success are another central component of comprehensive programming.3)
Social support: Comprehensive approaches must move beyond health and education to address the social contexts and environments in which an adolescent resides. Social support mechanisms and social skill development have been found to be critical for positive adolescent and youth outcomes. 10

To meet our definition of comprehensive, programs therefore had to include one or more of the following: social and/or vocational supports, family and/or parent engagement, Life Skills training, Family life and/or sexuality education.

Additionally, programs were only included if they had documented effects through outcome or impact evaluations. Such evaluations had to have been conducted using either randomized experimental, quasi-experimental, or pre- and post outcome measurement design with a sample size minimum of 100. For the purpose of this review, several studies could be included that corresponded to the same program.

sec.title: Outcome measures
sec.p: The primary outcome of interest was sexual and reproductive health including: adolescent pregnancy, contraceptive use, sexually transmitted infections, romantic relationships engagement and quality, sexual negotiation, and gender norms. Secondary outcomes included educational (school enrolment, retention, graduation rates, academic grades, college enrolment) and other health-related outcomes (mental health, social and/or emotional supports and networks, life-skills, employment, housing).

sec.title: Information sources and search strategy
sec.p: The following electronic databases were used: PubMed, Global Health, ERIC, Scopus, PsycInfo, MEDLINE, EMBASE, CAB, BIOSIS, POPLINE, Latin American and Caribbean Health Sciences Information (LILACS), the Pan-American Health Organization library catalogue (PAHO). We also searched WHO on-line databases: Index Medicus for the WHO Eastern Mediterranean Region (IMEMR) and the African Index Medicus (AIM) (see Table 1 for listing of search terms).

For the grey literature, we screened web pages from major organizations working in adolescent health (list available upon request) and relevant in-country government websites. We also used Google to search for additional information to determine program eligibility, which was not provided on organization websites. In cases where program data were not available online, we attempted to communicate with study authors or program staff.

sec.title: Data Collection and Analysis
sec.p: We screened titles and abstracts for peer reviewed and grey literature using an 11-item form for which a negative response to any item resulted in exclusion; all reasons for exclusion were recorded. Full text documents were obtained for all titles that passed the initial screen. Review of full text records resulted in exclusion of additional studies for failure to meet comprehensive criteria. All retained manuscripts were then subjected to a 56-item instrument used to extract information on 1) general study characteristics such as design, setting, population; 2) type of adolescent health program, outcome measures and results; and 3) evaluation design and quality.

Two independent reviewers (AK, JP) screened each title and abstract. Rating inconsistencies or disagreements were resolved through consultation with a third reviewer (RB). The same two reviewers extracted data from all included studies, and a third reviewer (ST) assisted by contacting programs for additional information when needed.

Before initiating the search process, all instruments were piloted on a random selection of 50 references encompassing the entire time period (1998-2013) to assess inter-observer reliability and identify gaps in screening and data extraction forms.

sec.title: Assessment of risk of bias in individual studies
sec.p: We evaluated each included study for methodological quality using Higgins (2011) principles including: random selections of participations, random allocations of the intervention, blinding, attrition, intervention and control group crossover, sample size, duration of follow-up and selective reporting. 11

sec.title: Quality Appraisal
sec.p: Following data extraction, we assessed the evidence level of all programs using available evaluation data. We did not intend to conduct a formal outcome-level assessment of intervention effects and compare these in a meta-analysis because of the varied range of interventions and study designs. Rather, we conducted a quality assessment based on the extent to which the programs met the following criteria: 1) thorough description of all components; 2) use of conceptual framework or logic model; 3) replicated (copying the specific elements that made the programme successful and applying them elsewhere); 4) scaled up (either to a wider geographical or institutional scale, or integrated in regional or national policies); 5) detailed description of measures and outcomes; 6) significantly impacted outcomes; and 7) published evaluation results in a peer-reviewed journal. The program qualities were then categorized by evidence level: rigorous (experimental evaluation with random assignment of intervention and control groups), strong (quasi-experimental evaluation using pre-post, controlled assessment without randomization), and modest evidence (pre-post assessment without randomization or control group).

sec.title: Data synthesis
sec.p: We synthesized all extracted data into descriptive program characteristics and quality assessments. In addition, for programs with rigorous and strong evidence, we assessed the common elements (core activities that cut across several program models), challenges and lessons learned. In this process we synthesized data from several sources; results are thus presented for individual programs (not studies).

sec.title: Summary measures
sec.p: Due to the varied study designs and models we were unable to use a common summary measure for program outcomes. We therefore chose not to present any numeric effect estimates such as Odds Ratios or differences in means in the results section. Thus, when discussing program effects we refer to whether programs were able to show positive, negative or no impacts on the desired outcomes.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: Figure 1 depicts the systematic review process of screening through data extraction and program inclusion. In total, we screened 36,119 records from both the peer- reviewed and grey literature. Full text was obtained for 524 titles of which 181 manuscripts were retained for data extraction. Studies on programs that did not have educational or sexual and reproductive health service components were excluded (n=343) during full-text review. The 181 retained manuscripts corresponded to 46 programs for which data were extracted; however, 27 programs were subsequently excluded because they either did not meet the comprehensive criteria or because of too much missing information on program components or outcomes. Additionally, nine programs that met the criteria were excluded due to insufficient evaluation, which typically provided for a pre-test only design. These nine programs are all listed in web appendix I. Ultimately, ten comprehensive adolescent health programs met all the inclusion criteria for this review.

sec.title: Program characteristics
sec.p: Table 2 provides a brief overview of the characteristics of the ten included programs (see web appendix II for additional detail). The earliest program started in 1984 and the most recent was initiated in 2006. Five programs were still active as of November 2013. Most (n=6) were located in the United States; four others were identified in Egypt (n=1), Ethiopia (n=2) and Mexico (n=1).

sec.title: Size and target populations
sec.p: The size of the programs ranged from 100 to 10,000 participants with one program (Oportunidades, Mexico) indicating an estimated reach by one or more activities of over 100,000 adolescents. 12,13 Age range varied in that some programs included both younger and older adolescents (e.g. ages 10-19 in Ethiopian Biruh Tesfa and Berhane Hewan) 9,14 while others targeted a narrower age-range (e.g., high-school students in Prime Time, USA). 15 Many programs were developed for specific populations: adolescents at high risk for delinquency (e.g. PATHS, USA), 16 early pregnancy (e.g., Prime Time), 15 or out-of-school migrant girls (e.g., Biruh Tesfa, Ethiopia). 14 Adolescents growing up in poverty were also frequently targeted. 17-19 Most programs were delivered to both males and females, but some (n=3) focused only on girls, specifically in rural, poor areas. 9,14,20-22 Two US-based programs (ASPPP and the Caring Equation) 23,24 were designed specifically for adolescent parents and their families. With the exception of Behane Hewan 9,25, developed for married adolescent girls and those at risk of early marriage, all programs included unmarried adolescents.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: Although many programs had similar goals, objectives varied from delaying age of marriage (Berhane Hewan) 9 to breaking the cycle of poverty (Oportunidades). 17 The most common objectives were teenage pregnancy prevention and sexual risk reduction. 15,23,26,27 Broader objectives to improve the overall health, wellbeing and social support systems of adolescents were also common.

sec.title: Activities
sec.p: Overall, programs used a wide range of activities and approaches. A common denominator was that many programs combined sexual and reproductive health services with in-school academic assistance or out-of-school educational supports, family life and sexuality education, and peer support/discussion groups. Some programs also included community mobilization, recreational activities and vocational training. Duration and intensity of activities also varied substantially; from a couple of months to several years, though in many cases this was difficult to determine because of limited reporting on activity frequency or concurrence of services delivered. Based on available information, few programs offered both long-term and frequent activities. The CAS-Carrera model is an exception, in which activities occurred daily (academic assistance, counselling), weekly (recreational activities, sexuality education) and annually (medical check-ups) throughout adolescence. 19,28 In contrast, Oportunidades focused on long-term economic supports (conditional cash transfers for health services and schooling) rather than regular activities for adolescents 17,29,30 and Prime Time 31 provided intense case management activities over a shorter period (18 months).

sec.title: Evaluation type and results
sec.p: Table 2 further presents the evaluation type used and corresponding results for each program. Three programs had been rigorously evaluated using a randomized, experimental design: CAS-Carrera, Oportunidades and Prime Time; five others had strong evaluation design using a quasi-experimental design: ASPPP, Berhane Hewan, Biruh Tesfa, Kansas Teenage Pregnancy Prevention, Ishraq; and two programs were assessed as having modest evaluations using a pre-post assessment without a control or comparison group: PATHS and The Caring Equation (see web appendix II for details on evaluation methods).

Programs with rigorous or strong evaluations were all found to have had direct or indirect effects on adolescent sexual and reproductive health. While these are described in detail in Table 2, examples of direct effects include increased contraceptive use in three US-based programs: Prime Time among females, 31,32 CAS-Carrera among females, 28 and ASPPP among males; 23 and reduced likelihood of pregnancy among girls (CAS-Carrera, ASPPP). 23,28 In Ethiopia, direct effects were seen through reduced likelihood of early marriage among girls (Berhane Hewan); 9 and in Egypt, Ishraq was found to change adolescent attitudes about early marriage (increased proportion desiring to delay marriage until after age 18) and family size (increased proportion desiring less than 3 children). 33 Oportunidades, on the other hand, appears to have had an indirect influence on adolescent fertility through increased schooling retention and graduation. 12

Programs both in the US and globally were found to improve school retention and graduation (CAS-Carrera, Prime Time, Oportunidades, Berhane Hewan). 9,28,30,31 So too, the Ishraq program, which targets out-of-school adolescents specifically, was found to improve math and literacy skills. 18 Additionally, results from Biruh Tesfa and Berhane Hewan in Ethiopia and Ishraq in Egypt showed that the programs improved adolescent girls’ peer social networks; Biruh Tesfa and Berhane Hewan also improved girls’ access to safe spaces. 9,14

For the two programs with a modest evaluation methodology (The Caring Equation, PATHS), 16,24 evaluation was restricted to only those who received the intervention; thus, the positive findings were difficult to ascribe specifically to the intervention.

sec.title: Program quality by evidence level
sec.p: Table 3 presents an assessment of the extent to which each program met the quality criteria by the three different evidence levels. Two out of three programs with rigorous evidence – CAS-Carrera and Oportunidades – met all quality assessment criteria including a through description of program components, measures, and outcomes; use of a conceptual framework or logic model; replicated and scaled up; demonstrated significant positive outcomes and published results in a peer-reviewed journal. Prime Time also met all criteria but had not been scaled up at a wider institutional level. Out of the five programs with strong evidence, two (Berhane Hewan and Biruh Tesfa) met all quality criteria but one, since it was unclear whether a conceptual framework or logic model exists that drove program activities.

Although programs displayed their results using clearly defined outcome measures, definitions and variables used varied. For example, the measure of contraceptive use ranged from ever use in Berhane Hewan 9 to consistency of use in Prime Time 32,34 and ASPPP. 23 Irrespective of their evidence level, most programs assessed short-term outcomes (less than 5 years); 35 those that were able to track the longstanding impact of activities (see for example CAS-Carrera) 28 were generally initiatives working with participants regularly throughout adolescence.

Finally, most programs with rigorous or strong evidence not only demonstrated a positive impact on intended outcomes, but had been replicated in at least one additional setting and/or scaled up to wider geographical or institutional levels. For example, Berhane Hewan has expanded from a pilot project in a single kebele (village) to reaching over 15,000 girls in three Ethiopian regions. 9,36

sec.title: Common elements of comprehensive adolescent health programs with rigorous or strong evidence
sec.p: Although programs employed substantially different approaches and activities, we also identified a set of common elements of comprehensive programs with rigorous or strong evaluations and positive impact on outcomes. These elements are summarized in Table 4. Overall, these programs share many commonalities such as use of empirically based and developmentally appropriate services and interventions, active engagement of young people, and the provision of skills to assure their success. Most centrally, they all reinforce the importance of building and sustaining long-term human relations and support systems.

What also becomes evident is that effective programs include one or more elements relating to positive youth development, 37,38 defined as participation in pro-social behaviours and avoidance of health and future compromising behaviours 37. The CAS-Carrera model, for example, built alternate support systems that parallel family structures for when parental supports are not available. 19,28,39 Berhane Hewan established social networks for married and unmarried girls. 9 Additionally, all programs had distinct elements of adolescent participation and leadership. In Prime Time, adolescents were trained as both peer leaders and educators. 32,40 Similarly, adolescent involvement through peer support groups was a key feature of improving social networks for the poorest and most marginalized girls targeted by Biruh Tesfa.14,22

Programs with rigorous or strong evidence also provided direct access to sexual and reproductive health services as well as daily or weekly academic assistance. This was especially evident in the CAS-Carrera model, which focused on surrounding adolescents with supports and services from daily tutoring and college preparation to personal crisis support and contraceptive counselling. 39 Both Oportunidades and Berhane Hewan provided incentives to keep adolescents in school; 9,12 and in the case of Ishraq, community members worked together to promote out-of-school girls’ re-entry into the formal school system. 33

Importantly, many programs strived to impact not only their participants, but also the families and communities where they reside (see for example the Kansas school/community model for preventing teenage pregnancy). 27 Building community support appears to be both a common and critical element of the most rigorously evaluated programs. The use of local women as program promoters was essential to negotiate girl's participation in Ishraq, 18,33 just as community conversations were key for Amhara villages in Berhane Hewan to reach collective decisions about resisting early marriage. 9

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic review of the landscape of comprehensive adolescent health programs inclusive of sexual and reproductive health services. Our results show that over the past 15 years a wide range of self-identified comprehensive adolescent health programs have been implemented in countries around the world; however, few have been sustained over a long period and fewer sill have been rigorously evaluated. One notable exception is the CAS-Carrera program where sustained supports and interventions were provided to participants over years and concurrently long-term impacts were measured using a randomized evaluation design. However, it is remarkable the number of programs excluded from this review due to a lack of evaluation including control or comparison groups. Clearly, as Kirby has noted 2, there are resource constraints. This becomes evident by the lack of geographic diversity among programs with rigorous or strong evidence, as most programs included in the review were based in the United States. Lee Rife et al. 42 suggest that the paucity of programs as well as complex evaluations in low and middle-income countries (LMIC) likely reflects the infrastructure and economic disadvantage of resource poor communities. While this may in part be the case, there are examples of outstanding and well-researched programs from LMICs that challenge this assumption (see, for example, Berhane Herwan). 9,25 Importantly, we recognize that alternatives to randomized controlled trials are necessary to fully understand the real life conditions under which complex, comprehensive programs operate. 41-43

When we look across the most effective and well-researched comprehensive adolescent health programs, we note that these differ substantially from each other in their approaches, activities, populations, objectives and scope. While this makes it challenging to directly compare results, it mirrors the diverse cultural, economical, social and political contexts in which programs are being implemented.

In addition, despite the differences, we also see a number of common elements that cut across comprehensive programs with rigorous or strong evidence (Table 4). It is beyond the scope of this review to say whether these are in fact the critical elements of effective programs and further research is needed. Nonetheless, given the empirical base on which they were developed, it reasonable to believe that these elements represent good starting points for development of comprehensive interventions.

It should be noted that disaggregating the relative impact of intervention elements remains a challenge in evaluating comprehensive programs. 2,3,41,48,49 However, the claim of some programs that one cannot separate the elements without destroying the entire model is empirically unfounded. We are aware of one program -- Berhane Hewan in Ethipioa – that is currently exploring the impact of each of its comprehensive components. 36 The lessons that will be learned will advance cost-effective programming not just there but globally. Whether in high income or LMIC, resource constraints should favour evidence-based programs but so too, they should favour rigorously evaluating each of the components of comprehensive programs to see if a more parsimonious (and less expensive) model might yield similar results. That work remains to be done.

So too, much work remains in effective replication. Repeatedly, the evidence suggests that replicating a high quality program is indeed a complicated process that requires a balance of fidelity to the original and adaptation to the demands of the new context. The CAS-Carrera program, for example, does not appear to have had the same impact when replicated outside of New York City that it has had in the original setting. 2,3

Compounding this is the fact that many programs do not have publicly accessible documentation of activities; consequently, it is not possible to replicate them. In the present review, we have excluded numerous programs due to insufficient or unobtainable information. If we are to learn from the work that has come before, there is a need for more complete program descriptions in both peer reviewed articles and the grey literature. Reporting key elements of the program's context, target population, specifics of the intervention, outcomes and how they are measured are crucial to better understand the impact of the programs as well as to guide the efforts for future replication and scale-up. Additionally, currently there is an idiosyncratic aura surrounding the concept of comprehensive, where the term means whatever the program implementer desires. We would concur with Oringanje et al, 48 that there is need to develop consistency of language and guidelines to allow international comparisons. Recognizing this gap, the World Health Organization will initiate a process to develop reporting standards for programs with a focus on maternal, sexual and reproductive health.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: While we used a thorough search strategy, we are invariably constrained by search terms and therefore, we may have inadvertently overlooked some programs. Secondly, we were limited by the information that was available through publications, the web and contacting program developers and evaluators. While every attempt was made to be thorough, the fact that we were not able to find some materials does not mean that they do not exist. However, given the effort that was made to go back to the original program implementers and evaluators, if such material does exist, it is highly inaccessible. Third, our search of the grey literature was limited to three languages as well as the web sites of major international organizations. It is possible that program descriptions and evaluations may be available in other languages or websites. Due to varied range of interventions and study designs, we did not conduct a meta-analysis of effect estimates. Because it was beyond the current review to assess program effects, all results should be interpreted with caution.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: The results from this review indicate that comprehensive adolescent health programs that combine high-quality sexual and reproductive and other health services with educational and social support mechanisms can positively influence adolescent sexual and reproductive health. Common elements such as long-term commitments to adolescents, building human connections, engaging key community stakeholders, and the use of skill-building activities cut across all rigorously or strongly evaluated programs. However, more research is needed to better understand the mechanisms behind program effects as the wide range of approaches used by comprehensive programs makes it challenging to directly compare results. Additionally, the longstanding impact of many programs regrettably cannot be proven due to insufficient evaluation designs. There is need both for program developers and the scientific community to recognize and use evaluation approaches that take into account the complex conditions under which comprehensive programs operate. Finally, there is need to identify key implementation factors that can aid in the scale-up of effective models.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors wish to thank Dr. Lale Say at the Department of Reproductive Health and Research at the WHO for her continuous support to the review process and for providing valuable input. Special acknowledgement also to the contribution of Claire Twose and Peggy Gross, information specialists at the Welsh Medical Library, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, who designed and implemented the search of peer-reviewed literature. Last but not least, we thank programme staff and evaluators who took the time to provided additional information on programmes: Ms. Enrica Bertoldo, Adolescent Family Life Programme Coordinator in California (ASPPP); Dr. Peggy B. Smith, Director of Teen Health Clinics (Baylor Teen Clinic); Dr. Jeff Randall, Assistant Professor, and Dr. Cynthia Cupit Swenson, Professor (Neighborhood Solutions); Ms. Abigail van Straaten (CAS-Carrera); Ms. Julie Strand, Development Director (Chicago Youth Programmes); Ms. Fleur Pollard at IPPF, Ishmael Selassie, Learning Center and Youth Coordinator at the PPAG Head Office, and Katie Chau, ASK Senegal National Programme Coordinator (Young&Wise); and Dr Stevenson, Programme Assessment Coordinator and Emeritus of Psychology (Project HOPE).

This review was conducted with financial support from the UNDP/UNFPA/UNICEF/WHO/The World Bank Special Programme of Research, Development and Research Training in Human Reproduction (HRP), Department of Reproductive Health and Research, World Health Organization.

fn.p: AK led the conceptualization of the study and coordinated the review, screened peer-reviewed and grey literature titles/abstracts, extracted, analysed and synthetized data, designed and drafted the manuscript content and structure inclusive of tables and figures.

JP helped conceptualize the study, screened peer-reviewed and grey literature titles/abstracts, extracted, analysed and synthetized data, participated in all decision regarding manuscript content and structure, and wrote parts and edited the manuscript including tables and figures.
ÖT helped conceptualize the study, participated in all decision regarding data analysis and synthesis as well as the manuscript content and structure, and wrote parts and edited the manuscript.

ST contacted programme implementers and evaluators for more information on programme components wrote the full synopsis of programmes included in the web appendices, and contributed to manuscript content, structure and revisions.

RWB helped conceptualize the study; participated as a third reviewer, participated in all decisions regarding manuscript content and structure, wrote sections of the manuscript, and reviewed and edited the final manuscript.
Anna Kågesten, MPH is a doctoral student at the Department of Population, Family and Reproductive Health, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Baltimore, USA
Jenita Parkeh, MPH is a doctoral student at the Department of Population, Family and Reproductive Health, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Baltimore, USA

Özge Tunçalp, MD MPH PhD is a Scientist at the UNDP/UNFPA/UNICEF/WHO/The World Bank Special Programme of Research, Development and Research Training in Human Reproduction (HRP), Department of Reproductive Health and Research, World Health Organization, Geneva, Switzerland Shani Turke, MSPH is a program specialist at the Bill and Melinda Gates Institute for Population and Reproductive Health, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Baltimore, USA

Robert William Blum, MD MPH PhD is the William H. Gates, Sr. Professor and Chair at the Department of Population, Family and Reproductive Health and Director of the Johns Hopkins Urban Health Institute, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Baltimore, USA
The current study did not involve human subjects and therefore did not require institutional review board approval.

element-citation.source: Population Council, West Asia and North Africa Office

element-citation.article-title: Prime time: 12-month sexual health outcomes of a clinic-based intervention to prevent pregnancy risk behaviors.

element-citation.source: Part 3: Revised Draft. UNICEF/LAC Core Indicators for MICS4 (and beyond) with Rationale and Sample Module
element-citation.publisher-name: UNICEF The Americas and the Caribbean Regional Office

element-citation.article-title: Berhane Hewan: Supporting Girls in Rural Ethiopia.
element-citation.source: Identifying and costing critical strategies that impact upon child marriage, schooling and reproductive behavior

element-citation.article-title: Positive Youth Development in the United States: Research Findings on Evaluations of Positive Youth Development Programs.
element-citation.source: The ANNALS of the American Academy of Political and Social Science

element-citation.article-title: Positive youth development: thriving as the basis of personhood and civil society. New directions for youth development.

element-citation.source: The Children's Aid Society. Carrera Adolescent Pregnancy Prevention Program

element-citation.article-title: Prime Time: a youth development approach to preventing negative sexual health outcomes.
A Review of Positive Youth Development Programs That Promote Adolescent Sexual and Reproductive Health.
What works to prevent child marriage: a review of the evidence.

element-citation.source: RealWorld Evaluation: working under budget, time, data, and political constraints
element-citation.publisher-name: United States of America: SAGE Publications

element-citation.source: Whatever it takes: Geoffrey Canada's quest to change Harlem and America
The Cradle through College Pipeline: Supporting Children’s Development through Evidence Based Practices. A document from the Harlem Children’s Zone

element-citation.article-title: Interventions for preventing unintended pregnancies among adolescents.
element-citation.source: The Cochrane database of systematic reviews

element-citation.article-title: Challenges of evaluating multilevel interventions.

caption.p: Overview of screening and data extraction process.

tr.td: 1) Population: Adolescents

(((((“Adolescent”[Mesh] OR “Adolescent Development”[Mesh] OR “Homeless Youth”[Mesh] OR “Child, Abandoned”[Mesh] OR children*[tiab] OR adolescen*[tiab] OR teen*[tiab] OR youth*[tiab] OR young adult*[tiab] OR Young women[tiab] OR young woman[tiab] OR young man[tiab] OR young men[tiab] OR Young adult[mesh])))
AND
2) Comprehensive programme

((comprehensive[tiab] OR integrated[tiab] OR multi level[tiab] OR multifaceted[tiab] OR multi component[tiab] OR multilevel[tiab] OR multifaceted[tiab] OR multicomponent[tiab] OR multidimensional[tiab] OR multi-dimensional [tiab] OR holistic[tiab] OR holistic health[mesh]) OR “Community Networks”[Mesh] OR youth development programme*[tiab] OR youth programme*[tiab] OR teen programme*[tiab] OR adolescent programme*[tiab] OR “opportunity programme”[tiab]))
AND
3) Health services

(adolescent health services[mesh] OR school health services[mesh] OR reproductive health services[mesh] OR Family planning services[mesh] OR Health promotion[mesh] OR (Health[tiab] AND (care[tiab] OR programme*[tiab] OR planning[tiab] OR service*[tiab])) OR Teen pregnancy[tiab])) AND (“Intervention Studies”[Mesh] OR “Programme Evaluation”[Mesh] OR “Programme Development”[Mesh] OR follow-up studies [mesh] OR (epidemiologic*[tiab])
AND
4) Evaluation of some kind
(study[tiab] OR studies[tiab])) OR (intervention*[tiab] AND (study[tiab] OR studies[tiab] OR programme*[tiab] OR pilot[tiab]) OR evaluat*[tiab] OR causal effect[tiab]))

caption.p: Summary of Characteristics of included Comprehensive Adolescent Health Programs

tr.th: PROGRAMS BASED IN THE UNITED STATES

td.italic: California Department of Health Services, Maternal & Child Health Branch

tr.td: To reduce rates of teen pregnancy among siblings of pregnant or parenting teenagers, given the high rates of pregnancy in this population
USA (California)
Ages 11-17. Brothers and sisters of pregnant and parenting teenagers (who participated in California's CAL-Learn or Adolescent Family Life program
~2,000 adolescents served yearly across 18 counties

□ Provision of health services in line with individual needs, including: family planning and abstinence counselling, access to quality RH care, transportation to health facilities, incentives to avoid sexual risk-taking□ Assistance in getting a medical insurance
□ Academic guidance, tutoring, and assistance with library research

□ Education about media messages on body image and sexual behaviour□ Advocacy at expulsion and court hearings□ Field trips and group activities to improve social skills and social competency□ Sports activities

Pre-post assessment with control group21 indicated reduced likelihood of pregnancy among participating teenage girls, and increased consistent condom use among males.The program also decreased the likelihood of truancy and gang participation among program teens21.
1984 – on going
To help teens develop personal goals and the desire for a productive future, in addition to developing their sexual literacy and educating them about the consequences of sexual activity
USA (started in New York City, expanded now to partnership sites in 13 additional states
“At-risk” male and female adolescents in Harlem
~4,000 young men and women

□ Reproductive health care including physical exams, testing, contraceptive counselling; specialty care as needed.□ Annual medical and semi-annual dental check-up□ Daily access to counselling and personal crisis support as well as weekly discussion groups
□ Daily academic assistance such as tutoring, homework assistance, preparation for tests and college applications.
□ Weekly “Job Club”□ Weekly Family life and sexuality education□ Music, dance, drama, and individual sports□ Community organizer who keep “track” of all adolescents

Randomized controlled trial43 showed that the intervention successfully prevented pregnancy and increased contraception use among girls (no difference among boys). Sexual knowledge increased for both genders.The intervention also had positive impacts on other health outcomes, employment, and social networks43.

td.bold: Kansas School/Community Model for Preventing Teen Pregnancy
td.italic: The Kansas School/Community Sexual Risk Reduction Replication Initiative; University of Kansas; University of South Carolina; funded by the Kansas Health Foundation.

tr.td: To reduce teenage pregnancies, to delay the age of first sexual intercourse, and to increase contraceptive use among sexually active teenagers.
USA (Kansas)
Primarily females and males ages 14-17, though sexuality education components were taught in grades K-12
65,000 contacts with K-12 students

□ Increased access to contraceptives through school clinics, increasing the number of supermarkets carrying contraceptives, and installing condom vending machines at gas stations and convenient stores
□ Mentoring and tutoring support by staff and volunteers. Specific components varied between counties.

□ Comprehensive, age-appropriate sexuality education from kindergarten to12th grade□ Sexuality education for teachers and parents□ Family communication□ Peer Support Groups□ Alternative after-school and summer programs
Pre-post assessment with control group22 showed increased condom use and decreased pregnancy rates in intervention areas (no change in control areas).

td.italic: Initially funded by Ohio Department of Health, implemented and funded by the Akron Children's Hospital and its Foundation

tr.td: Started in 1990, unclear if still on going
To provide at-risk adolescents and their families with the services, skills and contexts needed for young people to make healthy choices.
USA (Ohio)
Long-term program working specifically with socio-economically disadvantaged adolescents beginning at age 12, continuing through high-school graduation.
>140 adolescents completed the program
□ Healthcare and supportive counselling
□ Homework help and tutoring and assistance with college admission during weekdays
□ Family life and comprehensive sexuality education□ Vocational training□ Creative arts□ All activities provided during weekdays

Pre-post assessment using level of program participation as the independent variable indicated that positive change in educational, employment and criminal justice outcomes increased with participation44.

td.italic: University of Minnesota Prevention Research Center, funded by CDC's Prevention Research Centers Program, and additionally by NIH

tr.td: 1999—20042007—2008Program duration 18 months
To provide clinic-based adolescent development interventions to reduce sexual risk behaviours among girls at high risk for early pregnancy.
USA (Minneapolis and St Paul, Minnesota)
Ages 13-17 females with high-risk SRH behaviours; aggressive and delinquent behaviours, or school disconnected behaviours
>120 females
□ Case management in addition to clinical services including sexual and reproductive health care.
□ Case management including discussion and links to school support services

□ Peer leadership groups; monthly case managers meetings with girls to promote positive family and school involvement, skills to avoid sexual risk-taking, and other individual needs.□ Service learning groups (12-17 week courses), community service engagement)□ Peer education (12-23 week training) in topics such as conflict resolution, sexual decision-making, and healthy relationships.

Randomized controlled trial showed that the intervention successfully increased contraceptive use in intervention areas after one year45.The program also had a positive impact on high school graduation and college enrolment at 24 months follow up25.

td.italic: Department of Health and Human Services, implemented by Virginia Public Schools

tr.td: 2003 – on going
To educate adolescent parents about child development, foster parenting skills, keep adolescent parents in school, encourage educational and vocational training, and provide interagency resources.
USA (Arlington County, Virginia)
Ages 14 to 20, referred after a student drops out of school due to pregnancy.
>700 adolescent clients and/or their partners

□ Pregnancy testing and maternity counselling□ Primary and preventive health services (e.g. prenatal care);□ Nutrition information and counselling□ Referral for family planning services, STI/HIV test and counselling□ Mental health services
□ Educational and vocational training

□ Adoption counselling and referral services□ Family life and sexuality education□ Child care/child care referral□ Transportation and outreach services for families□ Financial, legal and housing counselling□ Domestic violence counselling

Pre-post assessment without control group indicated positive changes in parental behaviours (e.g. fatherhood involvement) and parental attitudes (e.g. increased empathy) 6 months after the intervention19.

tr.th: PROGRAMS BASED OUTSIDE OF THE UNITED STATES

td.italic: Implemented by Population Council in collaboration with the Amhara Regional Bureau of Women, Children, and Youth Donors included Nike Foundation, UN Foundation, UNFPA, USAID

tr.td: 2004 – on going
To establish appropriate and effective mechanisms to protect girls at risk of forced early marriage and support adolescent girls who are already married
Ethiopia (Amhara region)
Married and unmarried girls aged 10-19 years, living in rural, poor areas
>15,000 girls reached to date with activities
□ Supported costs for health service clinic cards□ Referred all participants in need for SRH services to special clinics

□ School retention incentives (e.g. materials and (uniforms) for both in- and out-of-school girls□ Out-of-school married and unmarried girls met weekly for informal literacy and other education□ Incentive (goat) promised to parents of girls who were not married before their graduation

□ Biweekly girl's clubs led by adult female mentor, with livelihood training for out-of-school girls.□ Community conversations about early marriage, girls’ schooling etc. to identify problems and reach collective decisions.

Quasi-experimental evaluation showed that the program successfully decreased the likelihood of early marriage, and increased spousal communication about family planning5.Results also showed that program girls were more likely to remain in school, make new friends and have safe spaces to meet friends5,46.

td.italic: Population Council in collaboration with the Ethiopian Ministry of Youth and Sport, Addis Ababa Youth and Sport Comm., Donors include Nike Foundation, The Turner Foundation, UN Foundation, UNFPA, USAID, and PEPFAR.

tr.td: 2006 –on going

To increase social networks and support networks for the poorest, most marginalized girls living in poor urban slum areas in Ethiopia, and to increase the levels of HIV knowledge and experiences with VCT.
Ethiopia (Addis Ababa)
Slum dwelling, out-of-school adolescent girls (10-19); e.g. rural-urban migrants
>50,000 girls reachedScaled up in 18 cities; 245 mentors in 92 meeting spaces.
□ Annual wellness check-ups/basic health care screenings at local facilities.□ Basic medical care including SRH services free of charge through an arrangement with government health facilities.
□ Literacy and numeration education 3-5 times a week

□ Girl's groups 3-5 times/week 30-hr life skills & HIV curriculum, and vocational support.□ Help getting ID cards□ Shelter and support for victims of sexual violence.□ Special program for disabled participants□ Soap and sanitary napkins are also provided

Quasi-experimental evaluation showed that the program increased knowledge of HIV and where to get tested for HIV9.Program girls were also more likely to have social support networks compared to peers in control areas9.

tr.th: Program core components (activity type, format and duration)

td.italic: Caritas Egypt; The Centre for Development and Population Activities (CEDPA); Population Council; Save the Children

tr.td: 2001—on going

To educate girls in literacy and basic academic subjects, to help them understand more about their community and their rights, and to introduce them to the public sphere through safe spaces, sports, and recreational activities.
Egypt (Rural upper areas)
Out-of-school 13–15-year-old girls in rural upper EgyptAdolescent boys 13-17 in the same areas.
Directly reached >3,000 girls and >1,700 boys in 54 villages, and >5,000 parents and community leaders

□ Helped girls obtain ID and birth certificates, which in turn were used to get health insurance□ Representatives of youth clubs and schools suggested using an Ishraq based health ID card recognized by providers at local health centres
□ Literacy and math courses twice a week□ Promotion of girl's re-entry into formal school system by age 18.
□ Village committees for community input/support□ Life skills curriculums for boys and girls□ Physical activity/sports for 13 months□ Livelihood skills□ Parental and community dialogues

Quasi-experimental evaluation showed that the program increased family planning knowledge and successfully changed attitudes about early marriage (increased proportion desiring marriage after age 18) and family size (increased proportion desiring fewer than 3 children)15.Result also indicated impact on skills in math, reading, literacy, and the extent to which participants developed social networks15.
To break the cycle of poverty and to improve the education, health, nutrition, and living conditions of those in extreme poverty
Mexico (Introduced in rural areas, now implemented throughout the country)
Family based, but evaluated specifically among 15-24 year-olds. Eligible families are included based on poverty and need assessment scores. Families receive stipends for children ages 9-21.
>5.8 million families
□ Health care service packages including clinical services for entire family□ Nutrition supplement program; fixed monthly money transfer for improved food consumption.

□ Conditional cash transfers provided for keeping children in school:1) Educational grants provided from grades 3-12, conditional on at least 85% attendance and finishing a grade within 2 years.2) “Youth with opportunities”, prospective career savings accounts for adolescents who finish high school before age.

□ Conditional cash transfers provided given that participants attend rotating health promotion talks on adolescence and sexuality, family planning and other topics.□ All cash transfers are provided on a monthly basis directly to the female head of the household

Randomized controlled trial showed short-term impacts on contraceptive knowledge and slight increase in contraceptive use among women aged 20-24 years7.The program was also found to successfully impact school enrolment and educational attainment14,50.

caption.p: Quality Assessment of Programs, by Evidence Level

tr.td: CAS-Carrera Pregnancy Prevention
Oportunidades (Progresa)
Prime Time

tr.th: STRONG EVIDENCEPre-post, controlled assessment without randomization (quasi-experimental evaluation)

tr.td: Berhane Hewan
Biruh Tesfa
Ishraq
Adolescent Sibling Pregnancy Prevention Programme (ASPPP)
Kansas Teen Pregnancy Prevention School/Community Model

tr.th: MODEST EVIDENCEPre-post assessment without control group (non-experimental evaluation)

tr.td: The Caring Equation
PATHS

fn.p: “Replicated” refers to copying the specific components that made the programme successful and applying them elsewhere. Replication can occur both in local, regional, national or international settings.

“Scaling up” is broader than replication - it refers to when programme components are rolled up at wider geographical or institutional scales, or integrated in policies at regional or national levels.

caption.p: Common Elements of Comprehensive Adolescent Health Program with Rigorous or Strong Evidence

tr.td: Health services inclusive of sexual and reproductive health
• Financial support (health insurance or clinic fees)
• Direct provision of sexual and reproductive health services (daily access to family planning, HIV/STI counselling and testing, pregnancy test)
• Indirect provision of sexual and reproductive health services (daily access to referrals as needed)
• Basic medical care (annual medical and dental check-ups)
• Mental health (daily access to counselling, personal crisis support)
Educational support
• Academic assistance (daily/weekly tutoring, homework help, guidance, college test and admission preparation)
• Conditional cash transfers (for school enrolment and retention)
• Out-of-school (weekly informal literacy and math education, promotion of re-entry into formal school system)
Social support
• Community dialogues or mobilization on programme topics (early marriage, pregnancy prevention)
• Life skills and livelihood activities (weekly discussion groups)
• Vocational and livelihood training (weekly
• Comprehensive sexuality education (for adolescents)
• Family, parents and teacher support (sexuality education, discussion groups)
• Recreational activities (daily/weekly access to creative arts, sports, music)
